figures.caption: FIG. 5. Nonlocal differential conductance G1 under various boundary conditions. In (a), t̄JN(S),α = 0.7t1 and δtJN(S),α = 0.5t1 for α = pz, dxz, dyz, while t̄JN(S),α = δtJN(S),α = 0 for all other orbitals. In (b), t̄JN(S),α = 0.7t1 and δtJN(S),α = 0.5t1 for α = dxz, dyz, dxyV↑, dxyV↓, while t̄JN(S),α = δtJN(S),α = 0 for all other orbitals. In (c), t̄JN(S),α = 0.7t1 and δtJN(S),α = 0.5t1 for α = px, py, pz, while t̄JN(S),α = δtJN(S),α = 0 for all other orbitals. In (d), t̄JN,α = 0.7t1 and δtJN,α = 0.5t1, while t̄JS,α = 0.3t1 and δtJS,α = 0.5t1 for all orbitals, respectively. The solid lines and shaded regions represent the averages and the standard deviations, respectively.

FIG. 1. (a), (b) Schematic illustration of the hopping integrals in the V2O plane described byHAM,1 andHAM,2, respectively. (c) FSs obtained by diagonalizing the full Hamiltonian HAM. The solid (dashed) lines indicate the spin-up (spin-down) FSs.

FIG. 6. (a), (b) Schematic illustrations of scattering processes when the distance between the second and third leads deviates from Wb3/LAM ∼ 2. (c) Zero-bias differential conductance as a function of the distance between the second and third leads.

FIG. 3. (a)–(d) FSs of the altermagnet tilted by 45◦. Panels (a) [(b)] show the quasi-one-dimensional [quasi-two-dimensional] FSs of spin-up electrons and spin-down holes, while (c) [(d)] show those of spin-down electrons and spin-up holes. The solid and dashed lines represent the FSs of electrons and holes, respectively, and the arrows indicate the directions of the corresponding group velocities. (e)–(h) Schematic illustrations of the possible scattering processes in the present device. In (e) and (f) [(g) and (h)], spin-up [spin-down] electrons are incident from the second normal lead.
